description_long: This object is a weathered stone guard stone featuring a sculpted animal, likely a bear or similar creature, lying atop a rectangular plinth. The animal is rendered in a somewhat stylized, blocky form, with its head and snout extending forward and its body resting along the length of the base. The plinth is constructed from large, roughly hewn stone blocks, some of which show visible cracks and signs of age. The surface of both the animal and the base is mottled with lichen, moss, and discoloration, indicating prolonged exposure to the elements. One side of the plinth displays a decorative zigzag pattern carved into the stone, adding a touch of ornamentation. The overall appearance suggests this object serves as a protective or boundary marker in an urban or historical setting.
description: A weathered stone guard stone with a sculpted animal atop a rectangular, cracked plinth, featuring lichen and a carved zigzag pattern on one side.
description_view_0: Front view showing the animal's head and snout projecting forward, with the plinth's blocky structure and surface discoloration visible.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the full length of the animal's body and the layered stone construction of the plinth.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the back of the animal and the stacked stone blocks, with visible cracks and weathering.
description_view_3: Opposite side view showing the animal's profile and the decorative zigzag pattern carved into the plinth.
attribute_location: Typically found at the entrance to driveways, historical sites, or along the edge of urban streets to protect corners or mark boundaries, often adjacent to pedestrian pathways or vehicle access points.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, resting flat on its base, with the animal figure facing outward toward approaching traffic or pedestrians.
attribute_wear_patterns: Surface shows significant discoloration, lichen, and moss growth, especially on horizontal surfaces and crevices. Edges and corners are rounded and chipped, likely from vehicle contact and weathering. The decorative carving is partially eroded, and cracks are visible in the stone blocks, indicating long-term exposure to the elements.
attribute_historical_markings: A zigzag decorative pattern is carved into one side of the plinth; no inscriptions or symbols are visible.
